intro: How you argue — and how you repair — says a lot about your relationship. Answer 6 honest questions to find your conflict style. Every style can work; the point is understanding each other's.

options.label: Keep talking until it's resolved
Step away and cool down first
Propose a concrete fix right now
Say how much this is hurting

questions.text: What you need most from your partner in a fight:

options.label: Stay in the room with me
Give me an hour, I'll come back
Agree on what we'll change
Acknowledge my feelings first

questions.text: After a disagreement, you feel done when…

options.label: We've said everything out loud
I've had quiet time and we've hugged
There's a plan so it won't repeat
I feel emotionally close again

questions.text: The hardest partner behavior for you is…

options.label: The silent treatment
Being followed room to room
Rehashing without deciding anything
Cold logic while I'm upset
Mention it the same day, directly
Wait for a calm moment to bring it up
Suggest a system so it stops happening
Explain why it makes me feel unseen
A real debrief once we're calm
Coming back softer, on my own
Doing the thing differently, visibly
A long hug and an honest 'I'm sorry'

talker.title: The Talker
talker.description: You process out loud and want it solved tonight. Nothing festers with you — you'd rather hash it out than let it sit.
talker.tip: Give a Pauser room to breathe before round two. Space isn't rejection; it's how they get fair.

pauser.title: The Pauser
pauser.description: You need space before you can be fair. You rarely say things you regret, because you don't speak from the heat of the moment.
pauser.tip: Tell your partner you're pausing, not leaving. A quick 'I need an hour, then I'm back' changes everything.

fixer.title: The Fixer
fixer.description: You jump to solutions because you hate seeing them hurt. With you, things actually change — you close the loop.
fixer.tip: Sometimes they need 10 minutes of 'that sucks' before they want the fix. Feel first, solve second.

feeler.title: The Feeler
feeler.description: You lead with the emotion under the argument, so you get to the real issue fast — past the dishes, to what actually hurt.
feeler.tip: Name the feeling AND the ask. 'I felt unseen — could you check in next time?' lands better than either alone.
